title: The Rise of Alex Ross Perry: From the Art House to the Mouse House | The Big Picture (Ep. 48)
format: Sean Fennessey talks to Alex Ross Perry about Golden Exits, the low-budget economics behind his five films, casting Ad-Rock, and how he ended up writing Disney's Christopher Robin.

films.title: Golden Exits
films.note: His fifth movie and the main subject. A seven-character Brooklyn ensemble shot with no handheld photography at all, the last stop in a three-part progression from Listen Up Philip (nearly all handheld) through Queen of Earth (50-50). He cast Emily Browning, Chloe Sevigny, Jason Schwartzman, and Adam Horovitz by pitching them characters before he'd finished writing.

films.title: Impolex
films.note: His first movie, made for $15,000 and written around people and locations he already had access to.

films.title: The Color Wheel
films.note: His second, made for $25,000 the same way.

films.title: Listen Up Philip
films.note: The first film he wrote without worrying about locations or budget first. Premiered at Sundance in January 2014; the anxiety after finishing it is what led him to Joe Swanberg.

films.title: Queen of Earth
films.note: A single-location, four-character film shot in 12 days for under $200,000, financed by Joe Swanberg. He was on the phone getting the Christopher Robin call while in the edit room for this one.

films.title: Christopher Robin
films.note: The Disney Winnie the Pooh movie he wrote after pitching himself into the job on the strength of a Teddy Ruxpin option that fell apart. He'd just come from watching a cut of it on the Disney lot.

films.title: Nostalgia
films.note: A script he wrote to spec for director Mark Pellington, who reached out over Facebook and asked for something unsentimental he could shoot on a fixed budget and schedule.

films.title: Happy Christmas
films.note: Joe Swanberg's own film, which he used to show Perry how he could produce and finance Queen of Earth.

films.title: While We're Young
films.note: Adam Horovitz's prior acting role, and the reason Perry sought him out for Golden Exits.

films.title: The Royal Tenenbaums
films.note: His example of what counted as a studio movie when he was in college.

films.title: Kill Bill: Vol. 1
films.note: Another studio movie from that same college-era benchmark.

films.title: The Life Aquatic with Steve Zissou
films.note: His example of the kind of $40 million Disney-backed film that no longer gets made.

films.title: Greenberg
films.note: His example of a $10 million Focus/Universal movie from that same era.

films.title: The Beguiled
films.note: Cited as Focus/Universal circling back toward that scale of filmmaking.

films.title: Phantom Thread
films.note: Cited alongside The Beguiled.

films.title: Brick
films.note: The Rian Johnson movie that excited his college generation before Johnson went on to make Star Wars.

films.title: Star Wars: The Last Jedi
films.note: Rian Johnson's studio film, cited as the scale his generation aspired to.

films.title: Fantastic Mr. Fox
films.note: His comparison point for the stop-motion Teddy Ruxpin movie he tried and failed to option.

films.title: Star Wars: The Force Awakens
films.note: He says his unmade Teddy Ruxpin pitch had essentially the same plot.

films.title: Pete's Dragon
films.note: David Lowery was finishing this when his and Perry's shared agent connected Perry to the Christopher Robin job.

films.title: Arlington Road
films.note: One of Mark Pellington's earlier studio films.

films.title: The Mothman Prophecies
films.note: Another Pellington studio credit.

films.title: Cheap Killers
films.note: His pick for the last great thing he'd seen: a 35mm Hong Kong action marathon highlight about two hitmen in a coded gay romance.

films.title: Home Again
films.note: The Reese Witherspoon movie he watched on the flight in, and, per Fennessey, a Ringer favorite.

The Life Aquatic with Steve Zissou.title: The Life Aquatic with Steve Zissou
